title: Hydrocarbons Law of 1943
description: President Medina Angarita's reform requires foreign oil companies to share profits roughly 50-50 with the state and unifies concession terms at a uniform 40-year duration; government oil income rises sixfold within five years, the first major step toward state control of the sector.
source_name: Wikipedia — History of the Venezuelan oil industry (cross-check)

title: Military coup overthrows Rómulo Gallegos
description: Officers led by Marcos Pérez Jiménez overthrow Venezuela's first democratically elected president months after he signed a sweeping land-reform bill, beginning a decade of military dictatorship.

title: Pérez Jiménez dictatorship overthrown
description: A military-civilian uprising ousts dictator Marcos Pérez Jiménez, who had relied heavily on oil revenue to fund urbanization and modernization spending, opening the transition back to democracy.

title: Punto Fijo Pact signed
description: Leaders of Venezuela's three main non-communist parties agree to respect election results and share power, founding the stable two-party democratic system that governs oil-revenue distribution for the next four decades.

title: Corporación Venezolana del Petróleo (CVP) created
description: Decree establishes Venezuela's first state-owned oil company, tasked with exploration, refining and trading hydrocarbons alongside the still-dominant foreign concessionaires; CVP later merges into PDVSA's structure.

title: Hydrocarbons Reversion Law
description: Law mandates that all oil-company assets, plants and equipment revert to the state without compensation when concessions expire in 1983, and requires companies to deposit 10% of assets as a maintenance guarantee — a key legal stepping-stone to full nationalization.
source_name: International Legal Materials — Cambridge University Press

title: Oil-boom windfall revenue surge
description: Quadrupled global oil prices following the 1973 Arab oil embargo flood Venezuela with fiscal revenue, fueling a decade of state spending expansion that later collapses under debt when prices fall.

title: Oil nationalization law signed
description: President Carlos Andrés Pérez signs the framework law reserving the hydrocarbons industry to the state, effective 1 January 1976; a companion law creates Petróleos de Venezuela (PDVSA) as the operating state company (see 1976 entry).

title: Oil industry nationalized
description: PDVSA created as the state oil company, nationalizing Venezuela's petroleum sector.

title: "Black Friday" devaluation
description: Collapse of the bolívar's fixed exchange rate after a decade of oil-boom overspending, ending Venezuela's era of currency stability and introducing exchange controls (RECADI).

title: Caracazo riots
description: IMF-backed austerity package (fuel-price and transport-fare increases under President Carlos Andrés Pérez) triggers deadly nationwide riots, a turning point in Venezuelan political economy.

title: 1994 banking crisis begins
description: Banco Latino, Venezuela's second-largest bank, is intervened after a run on deposits; the crisis spreads to 17 of 49 commercial banks (53% of system assets) by August 1995, costing the government roughly $5bn in rescue spending and exposing deep sector corruption.

title: Hugo Chávez elected president
description: Beginning of the "Bolivarian Revolution," which progressively expanded state control over the oil sector and the broader economy.

title: Chávez's 49 enabling laws
description: Using special decree powers, Chávez enacts 49 laws including the Ley de Tierras (land reform/expropriation authority) and a new Hydrocarbons Law raising royalties and asserting state control over PDVSA management, triggering business-sector backlash.
source_name: Venezuelanalysis (contemporaneous reporting on the Hydrocarbons Law reform)

title: April 2002 coup attempt
description: Dissident military officers briefly oust President Chávez amid opposition protests following his politicization of PDVSA's management; mass counter-protests and loyalist troops restore him to power within 47 hours.

title: PDVSA general strike (paro petrolero)
description: Opposition-aligned oil executives and the CTV union federation shut down PDVSA operations for two months; crude output falls to roughly one-third of normal, costing PDVSA an estimated $14.4bn in lost revenue and leading to the dismissal of 18,000 employees (40% of the workforce).

title: CADIVI exchange controls established
description: Fixed, government-administered exchange rate and currency-control board created after a national strike/capital-flight episode, beginning two decades of multi-tier FX regimes.

title: Nationalization wave
description: Chávez government nationalizes telecom (CANTV), electricity, and major oil-project stakes (Orinoco Belt joint ventures), reducing private/foreign investment in core sectors.

title: First currency redenomination announced
description: Government announces the bolívar will be redenominated 1,000:1 as the "bolívar fuerte," effective 1 January 2008.

title: Bolívar devalued under new two-tier exchange system
description: Government replaces the fixed 2.15 Bs/$ peg with a two-tier rate (2.60 for priority imports, 4.30 petrodollar" rate for others) — a roughly 50% devaluation at the weaker tier — as oil revenue and reserves come under strain."

title: Chávez dies; Maduro succeeds
description: Nicolás Maduro becomes president amid falling oil prices, setting up the collapse of the following decade.

title: Oil price collapse begins economic crisis
description: Global oil price decline sharply cuts Venezuela's dominant export/fiscal revenue source, triggering the hyperinflation era.

title: Economic Emergency Decree
description: President Maduro declares a 60-day economic emergency granting powers to intervene in companies and ration foreign-currency access amid triple-digit inflation and acute shortages; the opposition-led National Assembly rejects the decree, which the Supreme Court later upholds anyway.

title: Observatorio Venezolano de Finanzas (OVF) begins publishing
description: Independent economists start publishing inflation and economic-activity estimates after the government stopped regularly releasing official statistics — the main non-government data source for this period.

title: Selective default declared
description: S&P declares Venezuela in "selective default" after missing a 30-day grace period on $200m in bond payments; Fitch follows with a PDVSA default rating days later, marking the formal onset of Venezuela's sovereign-debt crisis.

title: Second redenomination + "Bolívar Soberano"
description: Government deletes five zeros and launches an economic recovery plan pegged loosely to the petro cryptocurrency; hyperinflation reaches roughly 1,700,000% in 2018 per external estimates.
source_name: Wikipedia — Hyperinflation in Venezuela (cross-check; figures widely cited across IMF/press)

title: Guaidó declares interim presidency
description: National Assembly head Juan Guaidó claims the presidency, triggering a period of intensified US sanctions on PDVSA and the financial system.

title: US sanctions on PDVSA
description: US Treasury sanctions the state oil company directly, blocking most US-dollar oil-export proceeds from reaching the Maduro government.

title: Nationwide electrical grid collapse
description: Failure at the Guri hydroelectric complex -- source of roughly 80% of national electricity -- triggers Venezuela's worst-ever blackout, cutting power across most of the country's 23 states for over five days, with rolling outages continuing for weeks; hospitals, water pumping, telecommunications and oil-sector operations are crippled, contributing to an estimated $800 million in losses and at least 43 deaths, underscoring the collapse of public infrastructure investment during the 2010s oil-revenue crisis.
source_name: Wikipedia — 2019 Venezuelan blackouts (cross-checked against contemporaneous Reuters/AP wire reporting cited therein)

title: De facto dollarization
description: Widespread informal use of the US dollar in daily transactions becomes the primary inflation-coping mechanism as bolívar confidence collapses further.

title: Third redenomination — "Bolívar Digital"
description: Central bank deletes six more zeros; hyperinflation (37 consecutive months) is declared to have ended at end-December 2020 per independent economist analysis.

title: Chevron granted OFAC license to resume operations
description: US Treasury issues General License 41 authorizing Chevron to resume limited joint-venture extraction with PDVSA following renewed Maduro-opposition talks, a first partial loosening of the oil-sector sanctions regime imposed in 2019.

title: Barbados Agreement — temporary US sanctions relief
description: US grants a general license easing oil, gas and gold sanctions for six months in exchange for electoral-process commitments; later partially reimposed in 2024.
